speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number six, an interview with Robert Ness on August the 24th, 1995. So you escaped from the march with two Poles.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, on the march. We went-- you know, you have to keep in mind that at that time already, you know, it's January, February. It's not the same German army in the same order.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we were walking around the road and the road was making a curve, and there were some guards in front and guards in the back, but there was no one on the side. I went into the-- run into a farm. And then two more Polish boys-- they were from Warsaw Uprising. And we're running and the three of us were sitting in a farm as it passed and passed and passed. And after it was quiet, a farmer came in and he was a shoemaker-- a Polish fellow around there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so he knew that we run away from the camp. But the two-- the two Polish boys went in one way and I-- the shoemaker took me in some other place. And they didn't even know that I was from a camp. They knew I was a Polish boy who was working for the German, and the German was evacuating west. And I got sick. And he was going on, and I got sick and that's-- that's the story. And that's what he told them. That's the bill of goods that he told them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was in this family. But there was a problem. The mother, grandmother was religious. What do you mean a Polish boy doesn't know religion, doesn't know anything, doesn't know prayers? It was-- the first three, four days was really terrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I got a hold of a prayer book, and I quickly learned-- quickly-- the prayers. And when the grandmother used to come to say goodnight, I used to kind of smell when she was coming. Boom. I used to go on my knees before the-- before the holy-- the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The cross?

speaker: interviewee
text: Virgin Mary. The Virgin Mary, cross myself, and you didn't feel well. Do you feel better? She gave me a little medallion and so forth. Yes, it's better. But in the meantime, slowly, I was regaining my health a little bit. But what happened was one day, I used to hang up my coat in the corridor. But you see, as I told you, my coat didn't have any markings. My pants didn't have any markings. But my-- my jacket, inside in the coat, was absolutely a cross that big and so-- it was just impossible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which indicated prisoner?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. That was indicated prisoner, you know? A red cross. Or you-- I didn't have the-- those stripes. And besides, even if I would have had stripes, I would have exchanged them somehow or other. I had an idea of running away when it comes to march and evacuations.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- I work outside, helping there. And it was cold, but I couldn't-- either the coat or no coat. I went without the coat. I couldn't move around to do some work with the coat over you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And suddenly, I come back. I see the jacket is gone-- the-- the coat is gone. And obviously, the jacket-- one day, what happened was, oh, you know, they kind of got suspicious, whatever it is. There was some other running away. And they burned my jacket and oh, no, no. Can't stay here and so forth. So that they-- the shoemaker comes next day and takes me to another place. He says I'll see what I can do to get you a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I come there and we come to his friend, who is a shoemaker, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's unreal. It's unreal. And I come into the kitchen, and who is sitting in the kitchen? Two Jewish boys-- shoemakers working on shoes from Stutthof-- from the same place I run away from.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they start-- they see me and they recognized me and I recognized them. And they start speaking in Yiddish to one another. I say, hey, listen. The lagerfuhrer is coming here pretty soon. You better get moving here. This is not a place for you. Get moving. Disappear in a hurry.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were still connected with the--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were talking in Yiddish. In other words, the camp stopped there. That's where they stopped.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were connected with the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. It was the same group I run away from. I run away from them in one place, and that was our final destination, whatever it is. They were going in circles, whatever it is. But it was the same group-- block six and nine-- that I run away from. 6, 9, and 13. And I don't even have time. I tried to get to my shoemaker to tell him, hey, I-- I-- I have to leave. And before that happens, that lager-- the blocker-- the blockfuhrer-- the German one that used to have 6 and 13 under his command-- he walks in there. He had six and nine. Takes a look at me. I said [NON-ENGLISH]. He said, no, no, [NON-ENGLISH]. No, no, no. [NON-ENGLISH]. No, no, no. And he says-- and I start giving him a song about having worked for a German staff and I got sick and he was evacuating further. Oh. Before he comes in, a little boy was going with him, about nine years old-- six, seven years old. He was the youngest inmate in the camp, a Polish boy from the Warsaw Uprising. But he was in block six and that little mumser knew me. Mumser means bastard.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he-- he started-- and he starts off the conversation, [NON-ENGLISH] or [NON-ENGLISH]-- I don't remember exactly what my name was at that time. [NON-ENGLISH]. Jew, what are you doing here?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I just look at him. I say, [NON-ENGLISH]. Keep quiet. [NON-ENGLISH]. A kind of asking him-- begging with my eyes-- and also saying those words, [NON-ENGLISH], keep quiet. Don't say anything. And [NON-ENGLISH.] Don't say anything. And the blockfuhrer comes in. And he looks at me-- looks at me and he says, [NON-ENGLISH]. I say, no, [NON-ENGLISH]. No, no. [NON-ENGLISH]. Mind you, this is the same one that-- that I addressed when I was sitting on the stone. And I tell-- I give him the song and dance about having worked for a farmer and that he's moving west. In the meantime, my shoemaker must have realized I'm in trouble. So he got a hold of his friend shoemaker and that guy comes in as we were talking, and he [NON-ENGLISH], and start-- takes him out. Come on, let's go and have a drink. Let's go and do. And he takes him out of the kitchen. And when he took him out of the kitchen, I started-- it's no use. You talk about the cats with seven boots or whatever it is and that. I started to run and walk and I came back to the people where I came from. And they were afraid to hold me so I went among farmers in the neighborhood for about a few weeks. Two nights in one place, a night in the other. Still was a Polish boy, but no camp. No nothing. And then I came back to them, and I stayed with them for another two weeks until liberation, which was March 11, 1945, but to show you-- Yusef Negvesky. I took my name Yusef Negvesky because, you know, it's-- it starts with an N and it's bare. So Negvesky. And I said, I am from Slonim-- around there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the bombing starts already and the-- the Germans are going out, the Russians are moving in. So they had to make prayers and they wouldn't start. [NON-ENGLISH] Yusef? Where is Yusef? So they got a hold of Yusef, and I was praying with them that the house should be preserved and so on. And then, on March 11, I go out and there is a Russian soldier in warm clothes. And I just-- it must have been around 10, 11 o'clock.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just fell on him and started to kiss him and hug him and kissing and hugging. I wouldn't be surprised a bit that he most probably would've come to-- to burglarize the place, or steal, or do something. But be that as it may, he-- it was-- I was crying and-- and it just-- and hugged him and kissed him. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was March 11. And I stayed there for another few weeks. And then I-- there was the Russian troops came in. There was a captain-- a Jewish fellow. [DRINKS] Talked to him. I didn't tell I'm a Jewish boy, because, you know, I was still paying-- staying there with the Poles. But there was one smart Jewish fellow in the Russian soldiers. Says, he's a Jewish boy. He said, no, no, no. I said, I'm a Pole. I am from [NON-ENGLISH] Slonim-- Negvesky.

speaker: interviewer
text: A Russian soldier's going, oh, Negvesky, huh? That-- that landlord-- that [NON-ENGLISH]-- that big landlord in Slonim around was let-- I got trouble.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, no, no, no. Nothing to do with him. I mean, it's craziness what-- what things can happen. I mean, I-- in Yiddish, you say, [YIDDISH]. Only owe the soul to God. I say, Negvesky. Now I got [INAUDIBLE] business with a Negvesky. Because I had to explain to how come I know Russian and so forth. So I was in Slonim, Negvesky. But I managed to do that and then I volunteered and I joined the Russian army, but that's a story in itself.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the end of the-- when the war ended--

speaker: interviewee
text: The war is still on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: The war is still on.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when the war ended, you were in the Russian army.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the Russian army. Absolutely. I ended the war as a victor coming to the Germany as not an occupier-- as an occupier, as a soldier. In maybe one place we to ask me why I know the Germans so well. I told them I am from the Wolgadeutsche.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the Volga Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Volga Germans. I mean, at one place there, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you briefly summarize-- I know you left for this country in 1950.

speaker: interviewee
text: I arrived in this country.

speaker: interviewer
text: You arrived here. Those five years-- just a summary of what--

speaker: interviewee
text: As they say in Yiddish, [YIDDISH]-- on one leg. It's-- it's-- I started-- I started in the army as a translator in a certain department. But then the division was demobilized and this particular captain was sent to Stralsund. And so he says, you want to go with me? I said, yeah, I'll go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went with him as the translator. He came to Stralsund. Before you know it, the commandant of the [NON-ENGLISH] took me away from him. I translated a few times. He liked the German and so forth, so I became the translator for one of the commandants.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he got into trouble because he was running around with a woman-- a Russian woman from the civil-- from the prior-- First World War that was a daughter of one of the white Russians. And the-- the military-- the NKVD didn't like that, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: And before you know it, he was kicked out. He was a no good son of a bitch, anyway. The guy was swearing right and left. He couldn't say a sentence without a-- without a swear word in it. And the Russian swear words with the mother, you know? With the FM stuff. But eventually you left the Russian army.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eventually, I left the place. I found-- my father found me. I found my father. And in 1946, I was supposedly going back to Poland to meet my father in [NON-ENGLISH]. He came from Germany to find me. He found me. It's a story in itself. And he went back and I went back by way of Berlin. And I came in Berlin, went through the French zone. By that time, there was no-- no wall yet. And from there, I went to Schlachtensee, which was a DP camp for-- in the American zone. And then I was debriefed by the CIC for quite a number of weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because I got in-- believe it or not, was involved in a lot of-- in the translation process, you know, from this one, that one. I even attended Wilhelm Pieck's funeral. You know Wilhelm Pieck, the communist leader of Germany? The one from Thalmann and Pieck?

speaker: interviewee
text: He liked Hiddensee, so he came to-- he was buried on the Hiddensee. It's a beautiful island in the Baltic, a little bit-- 20 kilometers north of Stralsund. So the funeral-- the big shot came. Grotewohl was there, too-- the prime minister of East Germany at the time. He was still a Social Democrat. Anyway-- but be that as it may, so I had pictures of all kind of stuff. So they-- and then they took us-- the CIC-- took us by train as American-- as employees of the Americans, through the Russian zone. They were with us in a different [NON-ENGLISH]. If anybody stops, nobody speaks Russian. Nothing, nothing, nothing until we came-- came across the border and we came to Frankfurt. That was in '47.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you were in Bavaria.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bavaria.

speaker: interviewer
text: From '47 to '50.

speaker: interviewee
text: '47. '50. It was schooling and so on. Went to school and entered the university, partly, and then in 1950--

speaker: interviewer
text: You came to Chicago.

speaker: interviewee
text: To Chicago. I came to New York-- I mean, to Chicago, yes. Because there was the relatives from my mother's mother that lived here that died in '47. But her brother was here, [NON-ENGLISH] Abrams, who was an attorney already, of many years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister was here by that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sister, she came from Italy in '47 with Aaron.

speaker: interviewer
text: She and Aaron survived in the partisans?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct. They were forced into ghettos. And then they went from the Vilna-- Ghetto Vilna to the partisan.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you began your studies here at Roosevelt University.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Roosevelt University. I finished in 1953. And then I went to the University of Chicago Law School and I finished that in '56.

speaker: interviewer
text: With a law degree?

speaker: interviewee
text: With a law degree. I got a JD, yes. And then I practiced law for about 16 or 18 years and then I was out of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then you worked as a controller, was it? Or in a business after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in a business. Yes, I was a manager--

speaker: interviewer
text: Manager.

speaker: interviewee
text: --of the collection department. That was already in the '80s.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you met your wife in Chicago?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I met my wife in Chicago, and she came on a visit from Israel in '55, just one year before I graduated from law school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she never went back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's a story in itself. You know, I came to pack-- help her to pack. We pack two suit cases and we started to pack the third on. We never finished. It's absolutely as true as can be. And we started unpacking.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have two children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, two daughters, Ronna Lee and Karen. And Ronna is married. She's Ronna Lee Ness-Cohn. They used their first name and the last name, Cohn-- C-O-H-N. And Karen is married, too. They are both engineers, Ronna and-- Ronna and her husband, David Cohn. And Karen is married to a lawyer by the name of Andrew Horberg. And she is a scribe-- Hebrew ascribe, basically. And they live in Indiana.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think that your Holocaust experiences influenced your view of life? Attitudes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think it has a tremendous-- first of all, I learned one of the basic stuff-- that you have to pay. That nothing comes free. That you have to pay a price for your good luck in whatever it is. Sometimes it's in heavy price and it's partly also from my [NON-ENGLISH]. It's true. But it's [NON-ENGLISH]. The-- the good fortune-- I paid the price for it. And-- and you have to-- the-- the people of the world have to know about it. It's not a question of, oh, [NON-ENGLISH]. And other Nazis that were so on-- and to-- to put him-- it-- it doesn't really matter whether one more is going to be convicted or whether he going to put in jail for another one. It doesn't mean anything, that. But the principle that for these kind of crimes-- for this kind of treatment of human beings-- there is no statue of limitation. We will get you down until you die and starve. And then maybe burn you on top of it. So be sure that you don't get up from your grave.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you said, paying the price, what is the price?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I kind of-- I was influenced by this poem. It's by Schiller-- 'The Ring of Polycrates,' believe it or not. And I kind of-- when I read it, it's a story about a king comes to visit another king. And he says, oh, I'm very lucky, and everything belongs to me in here. And he says, yes, but you know, you-- you-- still an enemy of you lives, so he might still upset-- and before he finishes it, there is a-- a runner comes from-- from the particular-- his general. And to telling him that-- and here, he brings the head of his enemy and it's all in a bag. And he says, well, it's really pretty good. He said-- but-- he says, you know, but your boats still have to pass the-- the craters and so on. And before he ends that, the-- the-- the-- the people are unloading from-- they're full of stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said, you know, you are pretty lucky, you know? And it's pretty good. But I'll tell you something. I was also a king and I had a son.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had an-- an heir. [NON-ENGLISH]. God took him away from me. [NON-ENGLISH]. I saw him die. [NON-ENGLISH]. My good fortune-- I paid what I owed. And he says, therefore, if you-- if you cannot-- if the gods don't want to tell you what the price is, throw away the richest things that you have-- what is most dear to you. And this-- this other king-- he takes off the ring from his finger. And he says, this is the most precious ring that I have. And he screws it into the sea. And lo and behold, the next morning, the-- the-- a fisherman comes. And he says to him, King, I caught a fish so big and so beautiful, I bring it to you as present so you can have it. And before that is finished, he gives it to the cook.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the cook comes in. And he says, King, how lucky can you be? You know, the ring that you are wearing-- it's whole. I found it in the bottom-- in the belly of the fish.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as he says that, he says to him-- that other one says, oh, I cannot be with you anymore. He says, the gods want your destruction. And off he went and shipped off. In other words, you pay the price. The survival-- it's-- with pain and suffering and blood. And family members were killed and other people were killed. And you weren't so smart and you weren't so sharp. A lot of it was just plain simple luck. In between, basically you couldn't be a [NON-ENGLISH]. You couldn't be smarter. I need to figure out this or figure out that. And even if you survived one action, you could have gotten killed next day. And if you didn't get killed next day, there was still another day. You had to take it day by day, hour by hour. And that's life, too. You have to take it step by step. You cannot make the plans for generations. And you just have to live that-- on this Earth. You won't be here for all this life. Somebody has to know about what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. This is a picture of my sister, Pola, the one that was killed. And my sister Lisa, that is alive and lives now in Northbrook, and myself. And it must have been taken-- I suppose I'm, here, four years old. So Pola must have been-- it's '33 so she must be 11 years old. And Lisa is about seven or eight years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Lisa is in the lighter--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the right, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the lighter dress?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Pola?

speaker: interviewee
text: And Pola is the dark hair. This is a picture of me when I was probably four years old or something like that. These are all pictures that were sent to my grandmother by my mother to-- for their families such they will have. And that's how we got them here when we-- when we came. This is a picture of the family maybe in 1937 in [NON-ENGLISH]. To the left is Lisa with the long hair. Then my mother is in the middle right there. And then Pola, the eldest one-- the one that was killed. And my father and myself at the end. This is a picture from the early 1920s. And it's-- all the way to the right is my Aunt Sarah that was killed with my mother on November 14, 1941. In the middle is her friend that went to Uruguay and then to Israel-- Yushinski, Rachel. And the other one is Rachel, my father's sister that died in Brooklyn. She came to the United States after the First World War. This is a picture of Stanislaw Zukowski, a neighbor of ours from Raczki. And also a friend of my father's, as a merchant together. And he was the one who asked whether or not there is a Nussbaum from Raczki when I entered the concentration camp in Stutthof. He was very instrumental and helpful in arranging for me for a job in the block six and helped me, also, with food and otherwise. And he send us a card. This-- this picture, he sent me-- sent to both of us-- to my father and me-- in 1947, I think. February or later. Here is an inscription on the other side in Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was taken where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland, in Raczki. This is a picture of me in Munich 1948 or '49 as a student in Munich. This is a memorial meeting of the surviving Jews of [NON-ENGLISH] and the surroundings. And that took place in Foehrenwald in 1948 or '49. On the back of the picture is written when it took place.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was a displaced persons camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Displaced person camp in Foehrenwald, not far from Munich. Second from the top row in the left-- that is me. Next to me is Arnold Kehr. He's now professor of mathematics at the Delaware University. On the bottom, the one that is just-- the two people that are holding that [INAUDIBLE] table. The one to the left with the hat, Karl Eilander-- he's a doctor in New York. Collings is here in Chicago-- the one sitting next to him to the left. And that girl in the fur is Collings's wife. And there are some other people from [NON-ENGLISH] and Raczki. This is a wedding of our daughter, Karen, to Andy Horberg. They are on the grass next to one another. And then next to them, standing, is my wife, Dalia, in the white. And I am to her left. And the rest of them are cousins and aunts and uncles-- the whole extended family that live in the United States. I would like to thank the Spielberg Foundation and the crew today that came out and were very generous with their time and suggestions and patience. And I am really very thankful and grateful for having the occasion to-- to put it on tape and and to have it preserved for the family, as well as for anybody who is interested in the subject matter. And I would like to introduce the people-- [BABY CRIES] I would like-- to my left is my wife, Dalia Ness. In her arms is little Tali-- Avital. Next to them is my mother-in-law-- Dahlia's mother. She's-- we just celebrated her 90th birthday. Sonya Wiener from Tel Aviv. She lives with us now here. Next to her is my-- our-- my daughter, Ronna-- Ronna Lee Ness-Cohn. She's been a joy since the day she was born and continues to be so. And in her lap is the youngest one, which is Ilan Meir. And he is named after-- after Ronna's grandfather-- namely, Mrs. Wiener's husband, who died a few years ago in Tel Aviv. And last, but not least, is Arielle. And Arielle is distinguished in the sense, too, that she was named after-- her middle name is Pola-- named after her Aunt Pola-- Great Aunt Pola that was killed in Slonim. And to the right of me is David, my son-in-law-- David Ness-Cohn. And the husband of Ronna Ness-Cohn. A delightful son that we got and a very nice, good man. Both are engineers.

speaker: interviewer
text: One is at Motorola. The other one is at AT T. The one to the right is at Motorola. And a very distinguished family. And the little one-- the little fellow, I mentioned to you already. He-- and I think we just covered the territory, didn't we? Tali, I mentioned.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I guess one thing that I can add, as-- as a daughter is that what my parents really gave me-- both from my mother, who's Israeli, and from my father-- is a deep love of Israel. And a very Zionist commitment to Israel. And I remember once in high school, my mother told me that a group was forming of children of survivors. And I told her that I really felt more like a child of an Israeli than a child of a survivor. And that's really more of what my parents have given me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, back to me? Well, as I was saying, it's-- Elliot, you did a nice job, and it's appreciated very much-- the time and effort you spend with us. And I think it was a good, hard piece of work. But I think was worthwhile and it will be preserved and people will be able to hear it, or listen to it, and see what it meant to survive. It was just luck-- sheer luck. A little bit of more luck. You had to pass a lot of experiences and we are here. And we are very thankful to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And thank you to Ari.

speaker: interviewee
text: And thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ari? Oh, absolutely. Without him, we wouldn't-- we wouldn't have it on the screen.
